segments.s128-g73: بیامد is simple past (3rd‑person sing.) 'came/arrived' and appears first by poetic inversion instead of the more neutral SOV order. گران here is an adjectival modifier 'heavy/formidable' placed before the noun for emphasis (a marked word order in poetry). لشکری is the noun 'army/host' (the -ی can make it 'military/army‑related'), and بربری is a postpositive adjective 'Berber' (ethnonym) modifying that noun; together the phrase means 'a formidable Berber host.'
segments.s128-g74: This is a noun phrase in apposition that further specifies the force: سواران is the plural noun 'riders/horsemen.' جنگ‌آور is a postpositive adjective/compound 'war‑bringing' → 'warlike/warrior' that modifies سواران. لشکری again qualifies them as military/troops; Persian allows stacking modifiers after the noun, so the whole means 'mounted, warlike soldiers (of the host).'
segments.s128-g41: شه بربرستان is a title phrase 'the king of Barbarestan' (Persian often places title before place). بچنگ is a fused prepositional/nominal phrase meaning 'into the clutch/grip of' (بـ + چنگ). گراز here names the captor as a brutish force/person (figurative), so the whole segment reads 'the king of Barbarestan into the grip of a brute.'
segments.s128-g42: گرفتار شد is a change-of-state/periphrastic past meaning 'became captured / was taken.' با marks accompaniment or agency here ('with' or 'by'), and چهل رزم‌ساز is a compound 'forty rāmz-sāz' literally 'forty war-makers,' i.e. fighters; the phrase means 'was captured with/by forty war-makers.'
segments.s128-g43: ز here is the poetic preposition 'from/through' indicating cause or source ('from the slain'). کشته functions as a collective noun 'the slain,' and زمین is the subject. گشت 'became' takes the following simile مانند کوه ('like a mountain') as its predicative complement.
segments.s128-g44: همان is a demonstrative 'that very' or 'the same' pointing back; شاه هاماوران is a nominal phrase 'the king of Hamavaran' (Hamavaran as place/epithet). شد is the verb 'became,' and ستوه is the adjective 'weary,' so the clause reads 'that same king of Hamavaran became weary.'
segments.s128-g45: به پیمان is an idiomatic prepositional phrase 'by/on the covenant' introducing the agreement. که introduces the content clause 'that ...'; کاووس را uses را as the definite object marker showing Kavus is the object, and با سران ('with the chiefs') indicates accompaniment—together specifying whom they will bring.
segments.s128-g46: بر here carries the sense 'to/toward' (direction to a person): بر رستم آرد = 'bring to Rostam.' آرد is the Classical/Persian present/third-singular of آوردن 'to bring' used to state the promised action, and ز هاماوران 'from Hamavaran' gives the origin.
segments.s128-g47: This segment is a nominal list joined by و 'and': سراپرده (the inner chamber/behind-the-curtain space), گنج ('treasure'), تاج ('crown') and گهر ('jewel'). The nouns are coordinated in typical Persian order with و between items.
segments.s128-g48: Another coordinated group: پرستنده as a noun 'worshipper/devotee' (here listed among attendants/possessions), و تخت 'and a throne', and زرین کمر is an adjectival phrase 'golden-kamar'—'a golden girdle/belt' (زرین modifies کمر). The adjective precedes the noun, and و links the items.
segments.s128-g1: دگر روز is a temporal fronting meaning “the next/another day.” لشکر is the direct noun (the army) placed before the verb, and بیاراستند is a past plural verb (they arrayed/they dressed up) in a classical/poetic form with the b- prefix; the subject is omitted and understood from the verb ending.
segments.s128-g2: درفش is the (direct) object/subject here “the banner/standard.” از دو رویه is a prepositional phrase indicating material/constitution or 'of/from two faces/panels' (از used to mark composition), and بپیراستند is another past plural verb (they adorned/they fitted) that links back to the actors implied by the previous verb.
segments.s128-g3: به هاماوران بود is an existential locative: به + place (هاماوران “the flat/level plains”) followed by بود (past of to be) = 'there was/there were.' صد is the numeral 'a hundred' modifying پیل (elephants); ژنده is an adjective-like modifier attached to پیل ('shaggy/old' in poetic use).
segments.s128-g4: یکی ‘one’ (a certain one) picks out a single example; لشکری here functions as an adjectival/attributive nominal ('belonging to/for soldiers' or 'a troop') combined with ساخته (built/constructed) to mean 'had a troop (or a soldierly structure) built'; بر دو میل is a locative phrase 'upon/over two masts/poles' (بر = on/upon, میل = mast/pole). The clause describes what that one bore or carried.
segments.s128-g5: از آوای گردان is an ablative/causal phrase 'from the sound/cry of the ranks/troop' (از = from, آوای = the sound/cry, گردان = troop). بتوفید is an archaic/poetic third-person past-form verb with the b- prefix meaning 'it spat/shot forth' (here applied metaphorically to the mountain), and کوه is the subject ('the mountain') appearing after the verb due to poetic inversion.
segments.s128-g6: زمین آمد is a verb phrase 'the earth came/rose' (زمین as subject + آمد past 'came'); از نعل اسپان ستوه is a causal/ablative phrase 'weary/from the horses' horseshoes' (از = from, نعل = horseshoe, اسپان = horses, ستوه = weary) where ستوه is a postpositive adjective describing the resulting state.
segments.s128-g7: تو گفتی is the reporting clause 'you said' (second-person past); جهان سر به سر is an idiomatic expression 'the world, all through/throughout'—سر به سر = 'entirely'—and آهن‌ست is the nominal predicate 'is iron' ('-ست' = is). The whole segment reports what the addressee asserted.
segments.s128-g8: وگر is a conditional/concessive connective 'but if' that introduces a counterfactual clause; کوه البرز is the subject 'the Alborz mountain,' در is the preposition 'in/within' and جوشن‌ست is the predicate 'is in cuirass/armored' (جوشن = cuirass/armor). The clause sets up a concessive hypothetical following the previous claim.
segments.s128-g17: چنین is an adverb meaning “thus/so.” گفت is a past tense verb (3rd person singular) ‘he said’ — here used with the following prepositional phrase. با + لشکر سرفراز is an address/indirect object meaning ‘to/with the proud army’ (با is used archaically to mark the addressee). The whole segment is a short reporting clause: “He said thus to the proud host.”
segments.s128-g18: که introduces the reported content (’that’/‘who’). از (here = ‘from’/‘off’) + نیزه (‘spear’) begins a prepositional phrase. مژگان is the object ‘eyelashes’; مدارید is a 2nd person plural verb form used here as an injunction (classical/poetic form) meaning ‘turn away/avert’ (the exact modern lemma is not listed in the provided senses). باز at the end is an adverbial particle (here functioning like ‘back/away’ or intensifier). Together the clause gives the command: ‘that (you) do not turn your eyelashes away from the spear.’
segments.s128-g19: This is a coordinate list of direct objects joined by و (‘and’): بش (an archaic/poetic item in the list), یال (‘mane’), اسپ (‘steed/horse’), عنان (‘reins/bridle’). بینید here is a past tense used in classical poetry for 3rd person plural (‘they saw’), so the verb applies to the whole list: ‘they saw X and Y and Z and W.’
segments.s128-g20: دو دیده (‘two eyes’) is the noun phrase. نهاده is a past participle/verb meaning ‘having placed’ or ‘set’ and here functions as a verbal adjective describing the eyes (‘two eyes set’). به نوک سنان is a prepositional phrase ‘on/at the tip of the spears’ (نوک = tip, سنان = spear). The whole is a participial clause modifying the sight: ‘two eyes set on the spear tips.’
segments.s128-g21: اگر introduces a conditional ‘if.’ صدهزارند is an archaic fused 3rd-person plural predicate meaning ‘they are a hundred thousand’ (ند is the verbal ending). The copula is omitted in the second clause, so ما صدسوار is elliptical for ‘we (are) a hundred horsemen.’
segments.s128-g22: فزونی لشکر is a genitive construction meaning ‘the army’s superiority/excess (in numbers).’ نیاید is a negative modal/verb (classical form) meaning ‘will not come/appear’ and with the idiom نیاید به کار it means ‘will not be of use.’ به کار = ‘to use/for the task.’ So the clause says that numerical superiority will not help.
segments.s128-g23: برآمد is a past verb ‘arose/appeared.’ درخشیدن is a verbal noun/infinitive ‘the shining/flash’ acting as the subject here (‘the flashing’). تیر و خشت are coordinated nouns (‘arrow and brick’ in the text) that are the items doing/undergoing the shining — a compressed poetic image: ‘the flashing of arrow and brick arose.’
segments.s128-g24: تو گفتی is the 2nd-person past ‘you said.’ The following clause is reported speech: هوا (‘the air’/wind) + بر زمین (‘upon the ground’) + لاله کشت. Here کشت functions as an action related to cultivation (‘sowed/planted’), so the phrase means ‘the air sowed tulips on the earth’ — a poetic image; note that some listed senses are nominal, but the context reads as a verbal/action sense.
segments.s128-g65: بسودابه is the speaker's name with the narrative subject not repeated; فرمود is the past-tense verb 'said/commanded' (3rd person past). کاندر here functions as a locative adverb/pronoun 'in/there' (classical fused form), followed by نشین in the imperative sense 'sit' — together yielding reported speech 'said, “sit there.”'. Word order places the reported command after the reporting verb.
segments.s128-g66: نشست is the simple past 'sat' (3rd person). و is the coordinating conjunction 'and'. به introduces the indirect object/target 'to' (here marking the thing praised). خورشید is the object 'the sun'. کرد is the past-tense verb 'did/made' used with the noun آفرین to form 'made praise' = 'praised'. آفرین is a noun 'praise/acclamation' (so کرد آفرین = 'praised').
segments.s128-g67: به لشکرگه is a prepositional phrase marking destination ('to the army‑camp') placed before the verb for emphasis. آورد is the past-tense verb 'brought' (3rd person). لشکر is the direct object 'the army'. ز شهر is the source phrase 'from the city' (ز = poetic 'from'). The clause reads 'he brought the army to the camp from the city.'
segments.s128-g68: ز گیتی is 'from the world' (ز = from, گیتی = world). بر + ین here combine as a classical/poetic marker (برین) meaning 'this/such' or 'supreme/aforementioned' modifying گونه ('kind, type'), so برین گونه = 'such/this kind'. جویند is the present-tense 3rd‑person plural 'they seek' (subject omitted). بهر is an adverb/preposition 'for/for the sake of'. The clause therefore says roughly 'from the world they seek such kinds for (their purpose)'.
segments.s128-g69: سپاهش is 'سپاه' (army) plus the classical possessive suffix ش = 'his/its' (so 'his army'). فزون شد is a compound 'grew/increased' (فزون 'increasing/greater' + شد 'became' in past). ز before the number indicates amount/extent: 'by/with three hundred thousand.' The structure is 'his army increased by three hundred thousand.'
segments.s128-g70: This segment lists types of troops: زره‌دار 'armored (men)' then و 'and' plus برگستوانور (an epithet/compound adjective in the text) followed by the head noun سوار 'rider/horseman'. In Persian lists like this the final noun (سوار) covers the previous qualifiers: 'armored and [epithet] horsemen.'
segments.s128-g71: برو here functions as a narrative/temporal particle 'thereupon/then' (derived from an imperative form in older usage). انجمن شد is 'an assembly/meeting was formed' (انجمن = assembly; شد = became/was formed). ز introduces the source or type: 'of Berber horsemen' (ز بربر سوار = 'of Berber riders'). The clause means 'thereupon an assembly of Berber riders was formed.'
segments.s128-g72: This gives the provenance and number: ز مصر 'from Egypt' و 'and' ز هاماوران 'from the plains' followed by صدهزار (numeral) 'one hundred thousand.' The preposition ز is repeated for each source; the number quantifies the assembled riders.
segments.s128-g33: This is an object‑first, verb‑last phrase typical of epic Persian: میانش (miānash) ‘its middle’ is the direct locus, به حلقه is a directional/goal phrase ‘into/onto a ring’, and درآورد گرد is a compound verbal idea where درآورد (made/caused) takes گرد ‘around’ after it — i.e. ‘made [a ring] around’. درآورد is a past/action verb (he made/put).
segments.s128-g34: تو گفتی is a short reporting clause ‘you said’ (subject + past verb). خم اندر میانش فسرد follows as the reported content: خم ‘the bend/curve’ is the theme, اندر میانش ‘in its middle’ is a locative phrase, and فسرد is a past/pret. form of the verb ‘interpret/expound’ used here as the reported predicate. Word order places the thing being interpreted before the verb, as is common in poetry.
segments.s128-g35: ز زین برگرفتش begins with the poetic preposition ز ‘from’, followed by زین ‘saddle’ and برگرفتش (برگرفت + ـش) ‘he seized/took him off’. The clause ends with به کردار گوی ‘in the manner of a ball’ (به + کردار = ‘in the manner/behaviour’, گوی ‘ball’), where به + noun expresses likeness (‘as/like’). برگرفتش is a perfect/past action with the object enclitic ـش ‘him’.
segments.s128-g36: چو here is a simile marker ‘as/like’. چوگان ‘polo mallet’ is the image, followed by the directional phrase به زخم اندر آمد ‘came into/struck the wound’ where به + noun = ‘into/toward’, اندر = ‘in/within’, and آمد is the past verb ‘came’. بدوی at the end is an archaic/poetic adjective/adverbial form; here it intensifies or characterizes the action (poetic quality). Word order keeps the simile and then the motion/result clause.
segments.s128-g37: بیفگند is a poetic preterite (he threw/cast) with an archaic prefixal form; و coordinates the next clause. فرهاد is the subject of the second clause, followed by دستش (دست + ـش) ‘his hand’ as object, and ببست (بست + prefix) is the past tense ‘he bound/tied’. Word order is subject (فرهاد) → object (دستش) → verb (ببست), a common SOV pattern.
segments.s128-g38: گرفتار شد is a periphrastic/resultative passive-like construction ‘became trapped / was captured’ (گرفتار = ‘caught/trapped’, شد = ‘became’). نامبردار is a substantive adjective ‘the aforementioned / the famed one’ modifying an understood person; شست here follows and is ambiguous in epic contexts but appears as a nominal (here taken as ‘thumb’). The clause therefore reports the capture of the named/famed man, with شست appended as a noun.
segments.s128-g39: This is a resultive image built with ز ‘from’ + nouns: ز خون خاک دریا شد literally ‘from blood the earth became a sea’ — خون ‘blood’, خاک ‘earth/ground’, دریا ‘sea’, شد ‘became’. The coordinate و links the second image دشت کوه ‘the plain (became) a mountain’, where the copular verb is understood from the first clause (ellipsis of شد in the second clause is typical).
segments.s128-g40: ز بس کشته افگنده از هر گروه is a quantitative/result clause: ز بس ‘from so many’ (بس used as ‘so many’), کشته ‘the slain’, افگنده (past participle) ‘cast/scattered’, and از هر گروه ‘from every troop/band’. Word order places the quantity and patient (کشته) before the participial predicate (افگنده), a common poetic arrangement to emphasize the slaughter.
segments.s128-g57: بیاراست is a past tense/preterite verb meaning "he adorned" (verb before subject is a poetic inversion). کاووس is the subject (the king Kavus). خورشید فر is a compound/epithet: خورشید (literally "sun" or "sun‑like splendor") modifies فر (royal glory); here it is the direct object/phrase being adorned.
segments.s128-g58: This noun group apposes or specifies the object/means of adornment. بدیبای رومی is a personal name + epithet ("Badibay the Roman"), یکی is the indefinite "a certain" (introducing the following noun), and مهد زر is a noun phrase "cradle of gold" (مهد = cradle, زر = gold). In classical Persian the link between phrases can be juxtaposition without an explicit verb, so this phrase supplies what the adornment involved.
segments.s128-g59: This is a series of adjectival/genitive phrases describing material and color: ز + پیروزه + پیکر = "a body of turquoise" (ز = "of/from" used poetically); then ز + یاقوت + گاه = "and at times of ruby" where گاه functions adverbially as "sometimes/occasionally." Word order is noun phrases following the poetic preposition ز.
segments.s128-g60: گهر ("gem/jewel") with بافته (past participle "woven") forms a passive/descriptive clause: "gems woven (upon) ..." بر is the preposition "on/upon," جلیل is an honorific adjective "noble/majestic," and سیاه is the noun/adjective "black." The phrase means "gems woven on noble black," with participle+prepositional phrase describing the surface.
segments.s128-g61: یکی is the indefinite "a certain," introducing the noun phrase. اسپ = "horse/steed." رهوار in classical usage is a travel/ride‑related noun (here best read as the mounted horse or rider associated with travelling), and زیراندرش is a fused adverbial/locative meaning "beneath it/under it" (the clitic -ش refers back to the horse). The phrase depicts "a certain steed, with a rider on/under it" in compressed poetic order.
segments.s128-g62: This describes the horse's headgear: لگامی (لگام + -ی as an indefinite/construct) = "a bridle," به زر = instrument/quality "of gold/with gold" (به used here to indicate material or adornment), آژده is an adjective modifying something on the head and here functions as "striking/terrifying" (poetic descriptor), بر سرش = "on its head" with the enclitic ش = "its." Grammatically, بافته etc. are implied elsewhere; this is a compact descriptive clause.
segments.s128-g63: همه functions as a distributive/totalizer "all/the whole," modifying چوب ("wood"). بالاش is the possessive/locative "its upper/above" (بالا + ـش), از is the preposition "from/of," and عود‌تر here names the material (agarwood/oud). The phrase reads "all the upper wood of it/from agarwood," i.e., its entire upper wood was agarwood.
segments.s128-g64: بافته is the past participle "woven" (used adjectivally/passively). The initial برو is irregular/poetic here and does not function as a straightforward modern verb "go"; it acts as a prefixed/deictic particle in older Persian constructions linking the participle to its surface (hence translated together as "woven upon it"). چندگونه = "of several kinds/various," and گهر = "gems." The unit means "gems of various kinds were woven (upon it)."
segments.s128-g9: پس is a narrative adverb meaning “then.” پشت پیلان is a genitival expression meaning “the backs of the elephants” (پشت = back (body) + پیلان = elephants). درفشان درفش is a noun phrase in which درفشان (an adjectival/participial form meaning “shining” or “glittering”) modifies درفش (“standard/banner”); the whole phrase describes the gleaming banner located on the elephants' backs.
segments.s128-g10: بگرد here is a poetic/older verbal form (jussive/preterite flavor) meaning roughly “was turned / was made to be” or “let (it) be turned”; it governs اندرون (“inside/inner part”). The three color words are listed coordinatively with و (“and”) connecting them: اندرون سرخ و زرد و بنفش = “its inner side [was] red and yellow and purple.”
segments.s128-g11: بدرید is a past verb (third-person plural or an impersonal past) meaning “tore.” The coordinated objects چنگ و دل شیر نر list what was torn: چنگ = “claw/talon” and دل شیر نر = “the heart of the male lion” (دل = heart + شیر نر = male lion), with the possessor following the possessed as is normal in Persian.
segments.s128-g12: عقاب دلاور is a noun phrase: عقاب = “eagle” followed by the adjective/epithet دلاور = “valiant/brave.” بیفگند is an older past-form of a verb meaning “cast” or “threw” (here intransitive/causative sense), and پر is its object “feather(s).” The clause therefore says the brave eagle cast off or shed its feathers.
segments.s128-g13: همی is a classical adverb that gives a continuing or habitual sense (here translated “kept on”/“were …ing”) and appears before the past verb بگداخت (an older past of گداختن) meaning “melted/was dissolved” or “dispersed.” اندر هوا is a locative phrase “in the air/sky,” so the clause means the clouds were dissolving in the air.
segments.s128-g14: برابر here functions idiomatically as “just/at that moment” (classical use); که is the conjunction “that/when,” and دید is the past verb “he saw.” ایستادن is a verbal noun/infinitive “to stand,” and روا is a predicate adjective meaning “fitting/appropriate” (the copula is omitted, as is usual in Persian). Together the clause means “when he saw, he judged that standing was fitting.”
segments.s128-g15: سپهبد is the subject noun (the commander, “spahbed”). چو here is the temporal conjunction “when.” لشکر به هامون کشید is a verb phrase: لشکر = “army,” به هامون = “to/onto the plain,” and کشید (classical past of کشیدن) means “drew/moved” (i.e., he moved/drew the army onto the plain). The whole segment reads “when the spahbed drew the army to the plain.”
segments.s128-g16: سپاه is the object noun “army/host,” here described by the numerative phrase سه شاه و سه کشور = “three kings and three lands/realms.” بدید is the past tense of دیدن, “he saw.” Grammatically the segment supplies what the spahbed saw: an army — three kings and three realms.
segments.s128-g49: بَرین (بر + ین) is an archaic/poetic grouping: بَر این 'upon these' — the demonstrative is fused. بر نهادند is the perfect/past 3rd-person-plural of نهادن (‘to place, to put on’), literally 'they placed/put (on)'. و (and) links the clauses, and برخاستند is past 3rd-person-plural 'they rose' or 'they got up / set out'. Poetic word order and compression place the demonstrative early and omit an explicit object; the subject 'they' is expressed by the verb endings.
segments.s128-g50: سه کشور is the numeral + noun 'three realms/countries' (numeral before noun). سراسر is an adverb meaning 'throughout' or 'entirely' and scopes over the following verb. بیاراستند is past 3rd-person-plural of بیاراستن (to adorn/array): 'they adorned' — the verb carries the same plural subject 'they' from the previous clause.
segments.s128-g51: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. از دژ 'from the fortress' is a prepositional phrase (از + دژ). رها is the verbal stem meaning 'free/release' and کرد is the past auxiliary 'did/made' forming the verb phrase رها کرد 'he freed'. کاووس را is the definite/direct object phrase with the object marker را; word order is inverted for poetic effect (the object comes after the verb phrase).
segments.s128-g52: همان here functions as 'the same' / 'likewise' (pointing back to the prior action). The sequence گیو و گودرز و هم طوس lists proper names, with هم before طوس meaning 'also/Tus as well'. The final را is the object marker applying to the coordinated list; the verb (răhâ kard) is understood from the previous clause and is omitted here (elliptical style common in epic poetry).
segments.s128-g53: A nominal list: سلیح (classical spelling of 'سلاح') = 'weapon/armament' is followed by coordinated noun phrases with numerals (سه کشور 'three realms', سه گنج 'three treasures', سه شاه 'three kings'). In Persian poetry numerals usually precede or follow the noun depending on rhythm; here they follow the pattern 'سه + noun'. There is no finite verb in this nominal enumeration — it inventories items.
segments.s128-g54: A continuation of the inventory: سراپرده 'inner chamber/behind the curtain', و 'and' links items, لشکر 'army', و تاج 'and crown', و گاه 'and gāh'. The list is paratactic (simple coordination) and omits verbs; each noun names an element of royal equipment/estate. گاه here is part of the list and retains its basic lexical meaning (a place/occasion/time) but functions as another nominal item in the catalogue.
segments.s128-g55: سپهبد is the subject 'the spahbed' (a high military title). جزین is the fused phrase جز این 'apart from this / aside from this'. خواسته is a past-participle/adjectival form 'desired / requested' modifying the following discourse; هرچ 'whatever' (هر چه) is an indefinite relative 'whatever/anything that', and دید is the past 3rd-person 'he saw' or 'he beheld'. The clause uses poetic compression: 'the spahbed, apart from this wish, whatever he saw...'.
segments.s128-g56: بگنج = به گنج 'into the treasury' (بـ- prefix + گنج), forming a directional phrase. سپهدار ایران is a genitival/epithet phrase 'the commander (spahdar) of Iran' (note Persian poetry often drops the ezāfe linking vowel in writing). کشید is the past 3rd-person singular of کشیدن 'to pull/draw/bring', here 'he drew/placed (into)'; the clause thus says he put/drew whatever he had into the treasury of the Iranian commander. (The verb is used transitively; the agent is the spahbed from the previous clause.)
segments.s128-g25: ز خون دشت is a poetic prepositional phrase meaning “from the plain’s blood” (the ezafe linking خون and دشت is omitted in verse). گفتی here is an archaic/poetic particle (not the noun “conversation”) with the sense of گویی/چون‌که — “it seemed / as if”; میستان is the predicate noun (“wine‑houses / places of revelry”), and شدست is an older past/result verb meaning “it became” (equivalent to شد + است). Word order: prepositional phrase → parenthetical particle → predicate → verb.
segments.s128-g26: ز نیزه is another prepositional phrase “from/through the spears.” هوا is the subject/scene “the air/atmosphere,” چون introduces a simile “like,” نیستان is the comparison term “a reed‑bed,” and شدست is the same archaic past/result verb “became.” The clause reads literally “from the spears the air became like a reed‑bed.”
segments.s128-g27: بریده is a past participle/adjective “severed/cut off.” ز هر سو is an adverbial phrase “from/on every side.” سر is the noun “head,” and ترک‌دار is an adjective attached to سر — here best read as “cracked / with fractures.” The whole group describes severed, broken heads lying about on every side (word order: participle → locative phrase → noun + adjective).
segments.s128-g28: پراگنده is an adjective/verb meaning “scattered / widespread.” خفتان is the plural/poetic form of ‘the sleepers’ (used euphemistically for the dead). همه is a quantifier “all,” and دشت و غار lists the places “plain and cave.” Order: predicate → nominal subject (the sleepers) → quantifier → location.
segments.s128-g29: تهمتن is an epithet/name (“Tahmātan / the mighty one”) functioning as subject. مران appears immediately after it as a proper name or appositive/vocative (the manuscript/lineation makes its exact apposition ambiguous). رخش را is the direct object with را marking definiteness (“Rakhsh” the horse), تیز is the adjective “swift/keen,” and کرد is the past tense verb “made/did” — together: “Tahmten (Maran) made Rakhsh swift.”
segments.s128-g30: ز خون is the prepositional phrase “from / by the blood” (i.e. with regard to bloodshed). فرومایه is a noun/adjective meaning “the base/low‑born,” and پرهیز کرد is the compound verb meaning “he abstained / refrained.” Grammatically this says he refrained (پرهیز کرد) with respect to shedding the low‑born’s blood (ز خون فرومایه).
segments.s128-g31: همی is a durative adverb meaning “continually / kept on.” تاخت is the past tense “galloped / rushed.” اندر پی is an idiomatic prepositional phrase “in pursuit of / after,” شاه is the object “the king,” and شام is a temporal adverb “until evening” placed at the end. Together: “He kept galloping after the king until evening.”
segments.s128-g32: بینداخت is a past causative/active verb “he threw / cast (down).” از introduces an agent/instrument or source “by / from,” and باد here is the natural noun “wind” (in poetry it can act as the instrument). خمیده and خام are two adjectival modifiers following باد (or an implied noun) meaning “bent” and “raw/unripe/untrained” respectively; the exact noun they modify is elliptical in the line. The phrase is therefore poetic/elliptical: “by the wind he threw down the bent, raw (one).”

sentences.s128-s37: A formidable Berber host arrived — mounted warriors, warlike troops.
sentences.s128-s21: The king of Barbarestan fell into the clutches of a brute and was taken captive with forty war-makers.
sentences.s128-s22: The ground became like a mountain from the slain; that same king of Hamavaran grew weary.
sentences.s128-s23: By covenant (it was agreed) that they would bring Kavus, along with the chiefs, to Rostam from Hamavaran.
sentences.s128-s24: The inner chamber, treasure, crown and jewel; a worshipper, and a throne and a golden girdle.
sentences.s128-s1: The next day they arrayed the army and adorned the banner with two faces (two panels).
sentences.s128-s2: On the level plains there were a hundred shaggy elephants; one of them bore a troop built upon two masts.
sentences.s128-s3: From the sound of the ranks the mountain spat (spewed), and the earth came forth, wearied by the horses' hoofs.
sentences.s128-s4: You said the world is entirely iron, but if the Alborz mountain is in armor...
sentences.s128-s9: He said thus to the proud host: do not avert your eyelashes from the spear.
sentences.s128-s10: They saw crest and mane, and steed and bridle; two eyes set upon the tips of spears.
sentences.s128-s11: If they are a hundred thousand and we a hundred horsemen, the army's greater numbers will not be of use.
sentences.s128-s12: The flashing of arrow and brick arose; you said the air had sown tulips upon the earth.
sentences.s128-s33: Sudabeh commanded, “Sit here.” He/she sat, and praised the sun.
sentences.s128-s34: He brought the army to the camp from the city; from the world they seek such kinds for their purpose.
sentences.s128-s35: His army increased by three hundred thousand — armored men and ... horsemen.
sentences.s128-s36: Thereupon an assembly of Berber horsemen was formed — one hundred thousand from Egypt and the plains.
sentences.s128-s17: He set a ring around its middle; you said, “Explain the bend in its middle.”
sentences.s128-s18: He snatched him from the saddle like a ball; like a polo‑mallet it drove into the wound.
sentences.s128-s19: He hurled him down, and Farhad bound his hand; the famed man was taken captive — (even) his thumb.
sentences.s128-s20: From blood the earth became a sea, and the plain a mountain; so many slain were cast down from every band.
sentences.s128-s29: Kavus adorned the sunlike farr with Badibay the Roman — a golden cradle.
sentences.s128-s30: Its body was of turquoise, at times of ruby; jewels were woven upon noble black.
sentences.s128-s31: A certain steed with a rider upon it; a golden bridle, a terrifying plume on its head.
sentences.s128-s32: All its upper wood was of agarwood; woven upon it were gems of many kinds.
sentences.s128-s5: Then, on the elephants' backs, the gleaming standard was set, its inner side red, yellow, and purple.
sentences.s128-s6: The talon and the heart of the male lion were torn; the valiant eagle cast off its feathers.
sentences.s128-s7: Clouds were dissolving in the air, and when he saw it he judged that standing was proper.
sentences.s128-s8: When the spahbed drew his army onto the plain, he saw an army of three kings and three realms.
sentences.s128-s25: They donned these and rose up, and they adorned the three realms throughout.
sentences.s128-s26: When he freed Kavus from the fortress, likewise he (freed) Giv and Goudarz and Tus.
sentences.s128-s27: Weapons — three realms, three treasures, three kings; inner chamber, army, crown, and seat.
sentences.s128-s28: The spahbed, apart from this request, took (or, wherever he wished) whatever he saw and put it into the treasury of the commander of Iran.
sentences.s128-s13: From the plain's blood it seemed the wine-houses had arisen; from the spears the air had become like a reed‑bed.
sentences.s128-s14: Severed heads lay on every side; cracked and scattered, the sleepers were all across plain and cave.
sentences.s128-s15: Tahmten (the mighty one) — Maran — made Rakhsh swift; he refrained from the low‑born’s blood.
sentences.s128-s16: He kept galloping in pursuit of the king until evening; by the wind he cast down the bent, raw (one).
